retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the role of TP53 in the etiology of Li Fraumeni syndrome. Explain the pathophysiology of Li Fraumeni syndrome. Summarize the Chompret criteria in the evaluation of Li Fraumeni syndrome. Review the importance of improving care coordination among the interprofessional team members to provide genetic counseling, medical care, and support services for patients affected by Li Fraumeni syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Initially, the diagnosis of Li-Fraumeni was done using clinical criteria based on signs and symptoms the patient and family have. With advanced genetic testing, patients can now know if they carry a copy of the TP53 tumor mutation before showing any signs of LFS. Genetic testing for TP53 mutations is very important. It is important to offer genetic counseling and testing to all relatives at risk of having a familial TP53 pathogenic variant. Genetic testing only detects approximately 70% to 75% of cases. Genetic counseling must be done to consider genetic testing, and if it concludes that a patient is carrying a copy of TP53 mutations, the patient must be counseled about surveillance and continuously screened for LFS related types of cancer. [23] The decision to do genetic counseling and TP53 mutation testing should be considered for the following patients:

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of developing multiple primary cancers is high in members of families with Li-Fraumeni syndrome compared to the general population. This excess risk mainly involves cancers characteristic of LFS, this risk is further increased in survivors of childhood cancers, and these patients require close monitoring for early cancer signs. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: There is currently no procedure or treatment option to repair mutations in the P53 gene as P53 is not a drug target yet. In general, radiation therapy should be avoided in patients with Li-Fraumeni syndrome as individuals are at high risk of radiation-induced secondary cancers. Similarly, exposure to computed tomography (CT) scans or X-rays should be avoided. [31] Li-Fraumeni syndrome is a complex syndrome with many implications for individuals and their families, so it is suggested patients with LFS or suspected LFS consult with a physician or health care system that has experience in treating patients with Li-Fraumeni syndrome and providing medical care, genetic counseling, and special support services. [19] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Referral to a genetic counselor is recommended to obtain a detailed family history, genetic counseling, discuss risks of cancer, and plan for cancer screening. [40] [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Not all individuals with Li-Fraumeni syndrome have TP53 mutations. It also can be due to an alteration in gene promoter which causes defective protein expression. It may also be due to new mutations that have not been described.

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Li-Fraumeni syndrome, also known as sarcoma, breast, leukemia, and adrenal gland (SBLA) syndrome, is a rare inherited autosomal dominant disorder associated with abnormalities in TP53, a tumor suppressor protein gene. This activity illustrates the evaluation and management of Li Fraumeni syndrome and reviews the role of the interprofessional team in improving care for patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Parents and patients should be informed adequately about all the signs and symptoms of cancer. They must be counseled to seek care whenever they have any unusual signs or symptoms that may be related to a cancer. Patients should be trained to perform a breast self-examination regularly every month, starting at age 18 years. Psychological monitoring is very important as these patients can develop anxiety or depression.

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The family history of cancer, young age at initial diagnosis, and multiple malignancies suggest cancer syndromes that are hereditary. Patients should be informed adequately about all the signs and symptoms of cancer. They must be counseled to consult whenever they have any unusual signs or symptoms related to cancer. A complete family history of multiple generations is very important due to a significant increase in cancer risk within families. [19] The history should include screening for multiple tumor types especially sarcomas, breast, leukemia, and adrenocortical tumors. [20] Sometimes risk becomes evident only after several years, so it is very important to update the family history regularly. Obtaining a thorough family history can be difficult, but it is an important part of evaluating every child diagnosed with an early malignancy. History should emphasize all cancers in the Li-Fraumeni syndrome spectrum. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 1969, a remarkable cancer predisposition syndrome was reported by Li and Fraumeni. [1] Li-Fraumeni syndrome (LFS) is an inherited autosomal dominant disorder that is usually associated with abnormalities in the tumor suppressor protein P53 gene (TP53) located on chromosome 17p13. [2] It is also known as the sarcoma, breast, leukemia, and adrenal gland (SBLA) cancer syndrome. LFS variants include LFS1, LFS2, LFSL. LFS1 is associated with mutations in TP53, a tumor suppressor gene. LFS2 is associated with mutations in CHEK2 (checkpoint kinase two), also a tumor suppressor gene. LFS-L are individuals that do not have detectable mutations in P53.

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Mutations in the core binding protein of TP53 have an increased risk of cancer. TP53 mutations have a high penetrance. The lifetime risk for women is 90 percent by age 60 years while in men the estimated lifetime risk is about 73 percent. [8] While most hereditary family cancer syndromes involve specific tumor types, members of LFS are at risk for a wide range of malignancies, with particular occurrences of breast cancer, bone sarcomas, and soft tissue sarcomas. Several other cancers have been reported but at a lower incidence in Li-Fraumeni syndrome. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Pathophysiology -- Mutations Associated with Younger Age of Onset in Li-Fraumeni Syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Short telomeres Genetic modifiers, including the MDM2-SNP309 polymorphism, and genetic anticipation P53-related cancers, usually breast cancer, soft tissue sarcoma, osteosarcoma (bone cancer), leukemia, brain tumors, adrenocortical carcinoma (cancer of the adrenal glands), and other cancers [16] [17] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with Li-Fraumeni syndrome can become resistant to chemotherapy. The risk of other primary cancers is increased if a germline mutation has occurred. There is evidence that a TP53 genetic mutation can cause a patient to have an increased sensitivity to ionizing radiation. Individuals with germline TP53 mutations should avoid or minimize exposure to diagnostic and therapeutic radiation when possible. [39]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with LFS are successfully treated during the initial cancer occurrence. However, radiation therapy should be avoided, when possible, due to several case reports and preclinical evidence showing an increased risk of radiation-induced cancers in these patients. [11] Li-Fraumeni patients are at a high risk of developing a second primary malignancy. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Li-Fraumeni (LFS) is a rare syndrome, as over 500 families with LFS have been reported in the literature. There are likely over 1,000 multigenerational families worldwide with LFS. Children with a diagnosis of adrenocortical carcinoma have the highest occurrence of P53 mutations. The syndrome is also associated with childhood brain tumors, osteosarcomas, and rhabdomyosarcomas. Patients with multiple primary tumors have a high incidence of P53 mutations. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Li-Fraumeni Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Hereditary breast and ovarian cancer syndrome (HBOC) Lynch syndrome Multiple endocrine neoplasia [35]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1608
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are classified as a form of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). This category is mapped to type IIa hyperlipidemia (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a condition that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern (Autosomal dominant inheritance). Recognizing that type IIa hyperlipidemia falls under the broader umbrella of lipid metabolism disorders and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians with diagnosis, genetic counseling, and the management of patients who present with elevated blood lipids.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 618
retrieved_docs.content: Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën is a subcategory within the broader classification of disorders of lipid metabolism. Within this hierarchy, فرط ليبيدات الدم (hyperlipidemia) is a child of Stofwisselingsstoornissen van lipoproteïnen en overige lipidemieën. The general group disorders of lipid metabolism is also linked to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (hyperlipidemia type IIa), which follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern. Clinically, Xanthoma due to primary hypercholesterolemia is mapped to disorders of lipid metabolism, illustrating a typical cutaneous manifestation of these lipid disorders. Understanding these relationships clarifies the classification, genetic basis, and clinical presentation of lipid metabolism disorders, aiding diagnosis and patient care.
